Layout Combinators with Propagation

elliot evans
 Apr 26 2026

Layout

Have you ever wanted to programmatically place things on a computer screen? I'm not talking about using a robot to slap sticky notes on the glass, I'm talking about lighting pixels up in certain places on the screen to display a graphic.

In many cases, the "basic" way to do that is to use a method that draws a graphic at certain coordinates on the screen. 

 draw(apple, 200, 200)

so yea, that's kinda what layout is for the purposes of this article. It's placing graphics at positions on the screen.

But layout gets more complex than rendering a single apple at a single spot. Layout is also about composing various graphics.

I want to show you an idea I have for how to do that.

Layout Combinators

You can combine a couple graphics that can be drawn at an x&y into a new combined graphic that can be drawn at an x&y! 
 above(beside(apple, orange), banana)

Implementing Layout Combinators

One reason I like layout combinators is they don't take much code to implement. Less code is nice because that way I don't mind re-implementing them in different languages or in different rendering environments. Less code is also nice because it makes them easier to tweak!

#circ_abov_c
const circle = (r) => {
    return {
        w: r*2,
        h: r*2,
        draw: (ctx,x,y) => {
            ctx.beginPath();
            ctx.arc(x+r, y+r, r, 0, 2 * Math.PI);
            ctx.stroke();
        }
    }
}
const above = (a,b) => { 
    const w = Math.max(a.w,b.w)
    return {
        w,
        h: a.h+b.h,
        draw: (ctx,x,y) => {
            const c = x+w/2
            a.draw(ctx,c-a.w/2,y)
            b.draw(ctx,c-b.w/2,y+a.h)
        }
    }
}

above(above(circle(10),circle(5)),circle(30))
  .draw(circ_abov_c.getContext("2d"), 20, 20);

Propagators

Its kinda like spreadsheet
a
b = a+1
= max(|a|,|b|) 
const ob = (v) => {
    const listeners = [];
    return {
    on: (l) => listeners.push(l),
    get: () => v,
    set: (newV) => {
        if (JSON.stringify(v) === JSON.stringify(newV)) return;
        v = newV;
        listeners.forEach((l) => l(v));
    },
    };
};
const to = (a, b, f) => {
    a.on((v) => b.set(f(v)));
    b.set(f(a.get()));
};

const a = ob(0)
const b = ob()
const c = ob()

to(a, b, (v) => v + 2);
to(b, a, (v) => v - 2);

to(a, c, () => Math.max(Math.abs(a.get()), Math.abs(b.get())));
to(b, c, () => Math.max(Math.abs(a.get()), Math.abs(b.get())));

[a,b,c]

Implementing Layout Combinators with Propagation

When we combinated graphics, we gave up the ability to draw the individual graphics at an x&y to gain the ability to draw the combined graphic at an x&y. We don't have to give that up though!

We can re-implement layout combinators with propagators. Now we are able to set the position of the middle circle directly (we couldn't do that before)!

#impl
const circle = (r) => {
    const p = ob([0, 0]);
    return {
    w: r * 2,
    h: r * 2,
    p,
    draw: (ctx) => {
        const [x, y] = p.get();
        ctx.beginPath();
        ctx.arc(x + r, y + r, r, 0, 2 * Math.PI);
        ctx.stroke();
    },
    };
};
const above = (a, b) => {
    const w = Math.max(a.w, b.w);

    const p = ob([0, 0]);
    to(p, a.p, ([x, y]) => [x + w / 2 - a.w / 2, y]);
    to(a.p, p, ([x, y]) => [x - w / 2 + a.w / 2, y]);
    to(p, b.p, ([x, y]) => [x + w / 2 - b.w / 2, y + a.h]);
    to(b.p, p, ([x, y]) => [x - w / 2 + b.w / 2, y - a.h]);

    return {
    w,
    h: a.h + b.h,
    p,
    draw: (ctx) => {
        const [x, y] = p.get();
        const c = x + w / 2;
        a.draw(ctx, c - a.w / 2, y);
        b.draw(ctx, c - b.w / 2, y + a.h);
    },
    };
};

const middleCircle = circle(5);
const entireGraphic = above(above(circle(10), middleCircle), circle(30));
middleCircle.p.set([10, 10]);
entireGraphic.draw(impl.getContext("2d"));

Going Further

Bluefish

alga

Optimizing propagation for groupings

By default, grouping and propagation can cause performance issues. For example say you have a situation where A → B → C and A → O and B → O and C → O; imagine O being something based on A,B, and C like O = max(A.get(),B.get(),C.get()). If you update A then this chain of events will happen:

1. A → B and A → O so B and O are updated
2. B → C and B → O so C and O are updated
3. C → O so O is updated

See how O was updated 3 times? We could have done better if we computed this situation artisinally by hand: instead updatd A, then B, then C, then finally updated O once at the end.

Its nice for Infinite stuff

Yea cuz you don't have to have a "root". I want to illustrate with a lil implementation of a natural number line or something.
